content.problemStatement:
Given a string sss and an integer kkk, Alice calls a string good if it follows the following condition


All the characters present in the string are of frequency kkk
Find the longest good substring of the string sss to make Alice happy.

String aaa is a substring of string bbb if it is possible to choose several consecutive letters in bbb in such a way that they form aaa.

Output the First such string that occurs.


Input Format

The first line will contain 222 numbers |SSS| ,kkk
The second line contains the string SSS


Output Format
Output the length of the longest good substring and on next line output the substring as well. If there is no such substring, print “IMPOSSIBLE” without the quotes.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and K (1 ≤ N, K ≤ 10^5) – the length of the string and the required frequency. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, if a good substring exists, output two lines: the length of the longest good substring and on the next line the substring itself (the first one in case of several with the same maximum length). If no good substring exists, output a single line with the word IMPOSSIBLE.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The substrings "aa", "aabcbc" and "bcbc" each have every character occurring exactly 2 times. The longest among them is "aabcbc" with length 6.
No substring contains characters each appearing exactly 3 times, therefore the answer is IMPOSSIBLE.

solution.approach:
For a substring to be good, every distinct character inside it must appear exactly K times. The number of distinct characters in a good substring cannot exceed 26 (lowercase alphabet). We iterate over the possible number of distinct characters d = 1 … 26. For each d we use a sliding‑window (two‑pointer) technique that maintains:
- cnt[c]: current frequency of character c in the window,
- distinct: number of characters with cnt > 0,
- exactK: number of characters whose frequency is exactly K.
When we extend the right end, we update cnt, distinct and exactK. If distinct exceeds d we shrink the left end until distinct ≤ d, updating the counters similarly. Whenever distinct == d && distinct == exactK the current window satisfies the condition; we compare its length with the best answer and keep the first longest one. The overall complexity is O(26·N) = O(N) per test case, and the extra memory is O(26).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (26·N in the worst case, but 26 is a constant).
